# Frontend Security Reference

Use this reference when handling user input, external content, links, or third-party integrations.
Security on the frontend is about defense in depth — the backend validates too, but the frontend should never be the weak link.

---

## Core Rules

1. Never trust user input. Validate and sanitize at every boundary.
2. Never render raw HTML from external sources without sanitization.
3. Never interpolate untrusted values into `href`, `src`, or event handlers.
4. Never expose secrets in client-side code.
5. Always validate server-side, even if you validate client-side.

---

## XSS Prevention

### Framework Escaping

Both React and Vue escape template expressions by default. This is safe:

```tsx
// React — safe
<p>{userInput}</p>
```

```vue
<!-- Vue — safe -->
<p>{{ userInput }}</p>
```

### Raw HTML Rendering

The one place frameworks don't protect you. Never use it with unsanitized content.

```tsx
// React — DANGEROUS
<div dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: userProvidedHTML }} />

// React — safe
import DOMPurify from "dompurify";

function SafeHTML({ html }: { html: string }) {
  const sanitized = DOMPurify.sanitize(html, {
    ALLOWED_TAGS: ["p", "b", "i", "em", "strong", "a", "ul", "ol", "li", "br"],
    ALLOWED_ATTR: ["href", "target", "rel"],
  });
  return <div dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: sanitized }} />;
}
```

```vue
<!-- Vue — DANGEROUS -->
<div v-html="userProvidedHTML" />

<!-- Vue — safe -->
<script setup lang="ts">
import DOMPurify from "dompurify";
import { computed } from "vue";

const props = defineProps<{ html: string }>();

const sanitized = computed(() =>
  DOMPurify.sanitize(props.html, {
    ALLOWED_TAGS: ["p", "b", "i", "em", "strong", "a", "ul", "ol", "li", "br"],
    ALLOWED_ATTR: ["href", "target", "rel"],
  })
);
</script>

<template>
  <div v-html="sanitized" />
</template>
```

Rules:
- Whitelist allowed tags and attributes — don't rely on blacklists.
- Strip `javascript:` URLs, `on*` event handlers, and `<script>` tags.
- If you only need plain text, use `textContent` instead of `innerHTML`.

---

## Link Safety

### External Links

```tsx
// Always add rel="noopener noreferrer" to external links with target="_blank"
<a href={url} target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">
  Visit site
</a>

// Why: without noopener, the opened page can access window.opener
// and redirect the original page (reverse tabnapping)
```

### User-Provided URLs

Never render a user-provided URL without validation.

```ts
// Shared validation — works in any framework
function isValidUrl(input: string): boolean {
  try {
    const url = new URL(input);
    return url.protocol === "https:" || url.protocol === "http:";
  } catch {
    return false;
  }
}
```

```tsx
// React
function SafeLink({ href, children }: { href: string; children: React.ReactNode }) {
  if (!isValidUrl(href)) {
    return <span>{children}</span>;
  }
  return <a href={href} target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">{children}</a>;
}
```

```vue
<!-- Vue -->
<template>
  <a v-if="isValidUrl(href)" :href="href" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">
    <slot />
  </a>
  <span v-else><slot /></span>
</template>
```
```

Dangerous patterns to prevent:
```tsx
// These can execute JavaScript if href comes from user input:
<a href="javascript:alert('xss')">Click</a>
<a href="data:text/html,<script>alert('xss')</script>">Click</a>
```

---

## Form Security

### Client-Side Validation Is Not Security

Client-side validation is for UX. Server-side validation is for security. Always do both.

```tsx
// React (Next.js Server Action)
export async function createUser(formData: FormData) {
  "use server";
  const parsed = CreateUserSchema.safeParse({
    email: formData.get("email"),
  });
  if (!parsed.success) {
    return { errors: parsed.error.flatten().fieldErrors };
  }
  await db.user.create({ data: parsed.data });
}
```

```ts
// Vue (Nuxt Server Route)
// server/api/users.post.ts
export default defineEventHandler(async (event) => {
  const body = await readValidatedBody(event, CreateUserSchema.parse);
  await db.user.create({ data: body });
  return { success: true };
});
```

### CSRF Protection

- Next.js Server Actions include CSRF protection by default.
- Nuxt automatically protects against CSRF in server routes when using `useFetch` / `$fetch`.
- For custom API routes, verify the `Origin` header or use CSRF tokens.
- Never mutate data via GET requests.

---

## Content Security

### Third-Party Scripts

```html
<!-- Load with integrity hash when available -->
<script
  src="https://cdn.example.com/lib.js"
  integrity="sha384-abc123..."
  crossorigin="anonymous"
></script>
```

### Iframe Embedding

```tsx
// Sandbox iframes that embed untrusted content
<iframe
  src={url}
  sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin"
  title="Embedded content"
  loading="lazy"
/>

// Never use sandbox="" without specific permissions — it's too permissive by default
```

### Image and Media Sources

```tsx
// Validate image URLs before rendering
function isAllowedImageSource(src: string): boolean {
  try {
    const url = new URL(src);
    const allowedHosts = ["images.example.com", "cdn.example.com"];
    return allowedHosts.includes(url.hostname);
  } catch {
    return false;
  }
}
```

---

## Secrets and Sensitive Data

### Never Expose in Client Code

```tsx
// React (Next.js)
// WRONG — "use client" means this runs in the browser
"use client";
const API_KEY = process.env.API_KEY; // undefined or leaked

// CORRECT — Server Component or Server Action
const API_KEY = process.env.API_KEY; // Server only
```

In Next.js:
- `process.env.SECRET_KEY` — server only (default).
- `process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_KEY` — exposed to client. Only use for genuinely public values.

```ts
// Vue (Nuxt)
// WRONG — exposed to client
const config = useRuntimeConfig();
// runtimeConfig.public.* is visible in client

// CORRECT — server only in server routes/plugins
// server/api/secret.ts
export default defineEventHandler(() => {
  const config = useRuntimeConfig();
  const secret = config.apiSecret; // Server-only, not in runtimeConfig.public
});
```

In Nuxt:
- `runtimeConfig.someKey` — server only.
- `runtimeConfig.public.someKey` — exposed to client. Only use for genuinely public values.

### URL Parameters

```ts
// Don't put sensitive data in URLs — they're logged, cached, and visible in browser history
// Wrong:
router.push(`/reset-password?token=${resetToken}`);

// Better: use POST body or short-lived server-side session
```

---

## Prototype Pollution

Be cautious with deep merge utilities that recursively assign properties.

```tsx
// Vulnerable — __proto__ can be injected
function deepMerge(target: any, source: any) {
  for (const key in source) {
    if (typeof source[key] === "object") {
      target[key] = deepMerge(target[key] || {}, source[key]);
    } else {
      target[key] = source[key];
    }
  }
  return target;
}

// Safe — skip dangerous keys
function safeDeepMerge<T extends Record<string, unknown>>(target: T, source: Partial<T>): T {
  const BLOCKED_KEYS = new Set(["__proto__", "constructor", "prototype"]);

  for (const key in source) {
    if (BLOCKED_KEYS.has(key)) continue;
    // ... merge logic
  }
  return target;
}
```

---

## Checklist

- [ ] No `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` (React) or `v-html` (Vue) without DOMPurify or equivalent
- [ ] External links have `rel="noopener noreferrer"`
- [ ] User-provided URLs are validated (protocol check, no `javascript:`)
- [ ] No secrets in client-side code or `NEXT_PUBLIC_` env vars
- [ ] Form inputs are validated server-side, not just client-side
- [ ] Third-party scripts use `integrity` hashes
- [ ] Iframes are sandboxed appropriately
- [ ] No sensitive data in URL parameters
